questions.question: What is Next.js?
options: A React framework for building full-stack web applications.
A CSS framework built for server-side styling only.
A database ORM maintained by Vercel.
A browser extension for debugging React apps.
questions.explanation: The official docs describe Next.js as a React framework for building full-stack web applications with features such as routing, rendering, and tooling built in.

questions.question: What is the quickest way to create a new Next.js app?
options: Install React manually and then add Next.js later.
Copy the Next.js source code into a local folder.
Create an app directory and skip package installation.
questions.explanation: The installation page recommends create-next-app as the fastest way to scaffold a new Next.js project with sensible defaults.

questions.question: In the official quick start, what do you do right after creating the app?
options: Change the bundler to Webpack first.
Change into the project directory and start the dev server.
questions.explanation: The quick start flow is: create the app, cd into it, run the development server, and visit the local URL in your browser.

questions.question: What local URL is used in the official quick start to view a Next.js app?
questions.explanation: The official quick start instructs you to visit http://localhost:3000 after starting the development server.

questions.question: What is the minimum Node.js version listed in the system requirements?
questions.explanation: The installation page lists Node.js 20.9 as the minimum supported version for the development environment.

questions.question: Which operating systems are explicitly listed as supported development environments?
options: macOS, Windows including WSL, and Linux.
Any mobile operating system with Node installed.
questions.explanation: The system requirements section names macOS, Windows including WSL, and Linux as supported environments.

questions.question: How does the documentation describe browser support for Next.js?
options: It supports modern browsers with zero configuration.
It requires manual polyfill setup for all browsers.
It works only when JavaScript is disabled.
questions.explanation: The supported browsers section says Next.js supports modern browsers with zero configuration.

questions.question: Which scaffold command behavior does the `--yes` flag enable when using create-next-app?
options: It forces Webpack instead of Turbopack.
It skips prompts by using saved preferences or defaults.
It creates the project without installing dependencies.
questions.explanation: The docs explain that `--yes` skips interactive prompts and applies saved preferences or the recommended defaults.

questions.question: Which features are enabled by the recommended defaults in create-next-app according to the docs?
options: TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, ESLint, App Router, Turbopack, import alias `@/*`, and AGENTS.md.
Pages Router, Babel, Sass, and Cypress.
questions.explanation: The installation page explicitly lists the recommended defaults, including TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, ESLint, App Router, Turbopack, the `@/*` alias, and AGENTS.md.

questions.question: If you choose to customize settings in create-next-app, which routing option can you choose?
options: App Router can be enabled or disabled.
You must configure routing manually after scaffolding.
You can only choose hash-based routing.
questions.explanation: One of the prompts asks whether you want to use the App Router, which is the recommended routing system in the docs.

questions.question: Which linter choices are offered in the installation prompts?
questions.explanation: The create-next-app customization flow includes a linter prompt with ESLint, Biome, or None as the options.

questions.question: What optional compiler-related prompt appears in the create-next-app customization flow?
options: Whether to use the React Compiler.
Whether to use the Rust TypeScript compiler.
Whether to compile with SWC or Babel only.
Whether to use the Edge compiler for all code.
questions.explanation: The prompt list shown in the installation docs includes an option asking whether you would like to use React Compiler.

questions.question: What does the `src/` directory prompt let you decide during project creation?
options: Whether to keep all dependencies in src.
Whether to place your application code inside a `src/` directory.
Whether static assets must live in src/public.
Whether routes are generated from the src root automatically only in production.
questions.explanation: The docs show a prompt asking if you want your code inside a `src/` directory, which is an organizational preference.

questions.question: What does the import alias prompt configure by default?
questions.explanation: The installation flow shows that the default import alias is `@/*`, and you can customize it if you want.

questions.question: Why might create-next-app include AGENTS.md according to the docs?
options: To guide coding agents to write up-to-date Next.js code.
questions.explanation: The recommended defaults mention AGENTS.md specifically as guidance for coding agents so they follow current Next.js practices.

questions.question: What is the main idea behind file-system routing in Next.js?
options: Routes must be registered in a central router file.
Routes are generated from the folder and file structure of the app.
Only JSON files can become routes.
Routing is configured only through next.config.js.
questions.explanation: The installation page explains that Next.js uses file-system routing, so the route structure is determined by how you organize files and folders.

questions.question: Which directory is required when manually setting up an App Router project?
questions.explanation: In the manual installation steps, the docs tell you to create an `app` directory because the App Router uses it for routes and layouts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `app/layout.tsx` in a manually created App Router project?
options: It is the root layout file for the application.
It defines ESLint rules for the app.
It is used only for API routes.
questions.explanation: The manual setup instructions identify `app/layout.tsx` as the root layout, which is required in the App Router.

questions.question: Which HTML tags must the root layout contain?
questions.explanation: The docs explicitly state that the root layout must contain the `<html>` and `<body>` tags.

questions.question: What is the role of `app/page.tsx` in the App Router?
options: It defines the home page for the route segment.
It is required only when using the Pages Router.
questions.explanation: The installation guide has you create `app/page.tsx` as the home page content for the root route (`/`).

questions.question: When a user visits `/`, which files are rendered in the manual App Router example?
questions.explanation: The documentation states that both `layout.tsx` and `page.tsx` are rendered when visiting the root route.

questions.question: What happens if you forget to create the root layout and then run `next dev`?
options: The build always fails until you add Babel.
The app switches to the Pages Router.
Routing is disabled until you restart the machine.
questions.explanation: The docs note that if the root layout is missing, Next.js will automatically create it when you run the development server.

questions.question: Why might you choose to use a `src` folder in a Next.js project?
options: It is required for TypeScript to work.
It separates application code from root-level config files.
It is the only place where components are allowed.
It disables absolute imports by default.
questions.explanation: The installation guide says a `src` folder is optional and can be used to keep application code separate from configuration files in the project root.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `public` folder in Next.js?
options: It stores static assets such as images and fonts.
It replaces the app directory in production.
questions.explanation: The docs describe `public` as an optional folder for static assets like images and fonts that can be served from the base URL.

questions.question: How do you reference a file named `public/profile.png` from your application code?
questions.explanation: Files inside `public` are referenced from the base URL. The docs use `/profile.png` as the example path.

questions.question: Which package installation command is shown for manually creating a Next.js app?
options: Install only `next` because React is bundled automatically.
questions.explanation: In the manual installation section, the docs instruct you to install `next@latest`, `react@latest`, and `react-dom@latest`.

questions.question: Why do the docs still tell you to declare `react` and `react-dom` in `package.json` when using the App Router?
options: Because the App Router cannot run without installing Vue.
Because they are needed for tooling and ecosystem compatibility.
Because Next.js does not include React at all.
Because they are only used for linting.
questions.explanation: The docs explain that the App Router uses React canary releases internally, but `react` and `react-dom` should still be declared for tooling and ecosystem compatibility.

questions.question: Which script starts the development server in a manually configured project?
questions.explanation: The package.json example on the installation page uses `next dev` for the `dev` script.

questions.question: Which script builds the application for production?
questions.explanation: The docs describe `next build` as the command that builds the application for production.

questions.question: Which script starts the production server after a build?
questions.explanation: The package.json example uses `next start` to run the production server after building the app.

questions.question: What does the `eslint` script do in the package.json example?
options: It generates the app directory automatically.
questions.explanation: The installation docs show `eslint` as the lint script for projects using ESLint.

questions.question: Which bundler is the default for `next dev` according to the installation docs?
questions.explanation: The docs state that Turbopack is now the default bundler for the development server.

questions.question: How can you explicitly use Webpack instead of Turbopack for development?
questions.explanation: The installation page says you can switch to Webpack by running `next dev --webpack` or `next build --webpack`.

questions.question: After starting the dev server, what does the documentation suggest you edit to see live updates in the browser?
questions.explanation: The run-the-development-server section says to edit `app/page.tsx` and save the file to see the changes reflected in the browser.

questions.question: How does Next.js add TypeScript support to a project according to the official docs?
options: You must install a third-party TypeScript plugin before the app will run.
Rename a file to `.ts` or `.tsx` and run `next dev`.
TypeScript only works if you create the app with Babel disabled.
You must manually write all Next.js type declarations yourself.
questions.explanation: The installation page says TypeScript support is built in. Renaming a file to `.ts` or `.tsx` and running `next dev` will trigger dependency installation and config generation.

questions.question: What file will Next.js automatically add when TypeScript is enabled in a project?
questions.explanation: The docs state that when you enable TypeScript, Next.js adds a `tsconfig.json` file with recommended settings.

questions.question: What minimum TypeScript version is listed on the installation page?
questions.explanation: The TypeScript setup section lists version 5.1.0 as the minimum supported TypeScript version.

questions.question: What extra editor support does Next.js include for TypeScript users?
options: A custom TypeScript plugin and type checker.
A browser extension for compiling TS in Chrome.
questions.explanation: The docs mention that Next.js includes a custom TypeScript plugin and type checker that editors such as VS Code can use.

questions.question: How do the docs suggest enabling the Next.js TypeScript plugin in VS Code?
options: Install a separate Vercel extension and restart the editor.
Choose “Use Workspace Version” from “TypeScript: Select TypeScript Version”.
Run `next ts-plugin enable` in the terminal.
questions.explanation: The installation guide tells VS Code users to open the command palette, choose “TypeScript: Select TypeScript Version”, and then select “Use Workspace Version”.

questions.question: Which linting choices does the installation page recommend at a high level?
options: You can use either ESLint or Biome.
Linting is discouraged in Next.js 16 and later.
questions.explanation: The linting section says Next.js supports linting with either ESLint or Biome, and you can run the chosen tool through package.json scripts.

questions.question: What is the recommended explicit ESLint config file mentioned in the docs?
questions.explanation: The docs recommend creating an explicit ESLint configuration, and `eslint.config.mjs` is the suggested modern format.

questions.question: Starting with Next.js 16, what changed about linting during `next build`?
options: The build always runs both ESLint and Biome automatically.
Linting was removed entirely from Next.js.
`next build` no longer runs the linter automatically.
Linting now requires the Pages Router only.
questions.explanation: The installation page notes that beginning with Next.js 16, `next build` does not run the linter automatically, so linting should be done via explicit scripts.

questions.question: If a project still uses `next lint`, what migration path does the docs provide?
options: Delete the lint script and rely on build errors only.
Switch the entire project to Babel.
questions.explanation: The linting section recommends a codemod named `next-lint-to-eslint-cli` to migrate existing `next lint` scripts.

questions.question: What built-in TypeScript and JavaScript configuration features does Next.js support for cleaner imports?
questions.explanation: The absolute imports section explains that Next.js supports the `baseUrl` and `paths` options in `tsconfig.json` and `jsconfig.json`.

questions.question: What does the `baseUrl` option help you do in a Next.js project?
options: Set the deployment URL for Vercel.
Resolve imports relative to a project directory such as `src/`.
Force all imports to remain deeply relative.
questions.explanation: The docs show `baseUrl` being used to make imports cleaner by resolving them from a root like `src/` instead of long relative paths.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `paths` option in `tsconfig.json` or `jsconfig.json`?
options: To map module aliases such as `@/components/*` to actual directories.
To configure static asset caching headers.
questions.explanation: The installation page uses `paths` to create aliases like `@/components/*`, which improves readability and avoids deep relative import chains.

questions.question: In the docs example, what does an import like `@/components/button` represent?
options: An absolute import alias pointing to a project directory.
A browser-only package provided by Chrome.
A required package from npm named `@/components`.
questions.explanation: The alias example shows `@/components/button` as a cleaner replacement for long relative import paths.

questions.question: How are `paths` mappings interpreted relative to configuration?
options: They are always relative to the public directory.
They are relative to the `baseUrl` location.
They are relative to the OS home directory.
questions.explanation: The docs explicitly state that each `paths` mapping is resolved relative to the configured `baseUrl`.

questions.question: Which statement about `jsconfig.json` is correct in the context of Next.js aliases?
options: It can be used instead of `tsconfig.json` in JavaScript projects.
It is only valid in the Pages Router.
It is required even when using TypeScript.
questions.explanation: The absolute imports section says that both `tsconfig.json` and `jsconfig.json` can be used for alias setup, depending on whether your project uses TypeScript or plain JavaScript.

questions.question: Which root layout signature shown in the docs is correct for an App Router TypeScript project?
options: A function that receives `{ children }: { children: React.ReactNode }`.
A function that must receive `(req, res)`.
A function that returns only a `<head>` element.
questions.explanation: The manual installation example uses a root layout function that accepts `children` typed as `React.ReactNode`.

questions.question: What is the simplest page content used in the manual installation example?
options: A dashboard with nested layouts and data fetching.
A login form with server actions.
questions.explanation:
The manual setup example creates a minimal home page that returns `

Hello, Next.js!

`.

questions.question: Which built-in component does the installation page use when demonstrating how to render an image from `public`?
questions.explanation: The public folder example imports `Image` from `next/image` to show how a static asset such as `/profile.png` can be rendered.

questions.question: If you manually configure scripts and want to lint with Biome, what kind of script do the docs suggest?
options: A script such as `npm run next-lint`.
questions.explanation: The linting section shows Biome scripts like `biome check` and `biome format --write` as examples.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of `next start` compared to `next dev`?
options: `next start` runs the production server, while `next dev` runs the development server.
`next start` installs dependencies, while `next dev` removes them.
`next start` is only for API routes, while `next dev` is only for pages.
They are identical commands with different names.
questions.explanation: The package.json example distinguishes `next dev` for development and `next start` for serving the production build.

questions.question: Which statement best matches the App Router note in the manual installation docs?
options: The App Router uses React canary releases built in.
The App Router requires React 17 exactly.
The App Router disables TypeScript support by default.
The App Router only works if you avoid layouts.
questions.explanation: The docs note that the App Router uses React canary releases built in, including stable React 19 changes and newer framework-validated features.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the `lint:fix` script shown in the manual package.json example?
options: To run the production server with auto-fixes.
To format HTML output in the browser.
To apply automatic lint fixes using ESLint.
questions.explanation: The package.json example includes `lint:fix` as `eslint --fix`, which runs ESLint and applies any automatic fixes it can.

questions.question: If you want to create a Next.js app without create-next-app, which approach does the docs call this?
questions.explanation: The official installation page has a dedicated “Manual installation” section for setting up a project yourself without the scaffolding CLI.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct reason to prefer create-next-app for beginners?
options: It automatically sets up routing, linting, and other project defaults for you.
It removes the need for React.
It only works for static export projects.
questions.explanation: The docs position create-next-app as the quickest path because it scaffolds a working project and installs the required dependencies automatically.

questions.question: What is the key difference between a relative import like `../../../components/button` and an alias import like `@/components/button`?
options: The alias version is cleaner and relies on configured absolute import settings.
The relative version only works in production.
The alias version only works in CSS files.
There is no practical difference between them.
questions.explanation: The docs use this exact before-and-after example to show how aliases reduce deep relative paths and make imports easier to maintain.

questions.question: Which summary best reflects the official installation page's recommended path for getting started with Next.js today?
options: Start with the App Router and let create-next-app scaffold a project with recommended defaults.
Begin with a custom Express server and add Next.js later.
Use the Pages Router by default because the App Router is deprecated.
Skip the development server and write only production config first.
questions.explanation: The official installation page centers the getting-started flow around create-next-app, the App Router, recommended defaults, and a fast local development loop.
